text: The surgeon uses a bipolar Maryland dissector to perform adhesiolysis, manipulating tissue with precision.
The surgeon angles the bipolar Maryland dissector at the appropriate angle using simple wrist movements, demonstrating the maneuverability of the instrument within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a bipolar energy device to coagulate small vessels, minimizing energy displacement compared to monopolar devices.
The surgeon addresses an incomplete fissure by first isolating and preparing to take the inferior pulmonary vein.
The surgeon switches between a suction instrument and a standard diathermy device at the patient's bedside, facilitating quick and easy instrument changes without assistance.
The surgeon uses Maryland forceps to dissect around the anterior hilum, precisely following the ideal tissue planes.
The surgeon uses bipolar fenestrated forceps to perform blunt dissection, manipulating tissue planes with precision.

The surgical field displays a variety of wristed instruments, including a bipolar Maryland dissector, bipolar fenestrated forceps, clipper plier, needle holder, scissors, and hook diathermy, all arranged for use in the robotic thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses bipolar fenestrated forceps to dissect around the inferior pulmonary vein, preparing the tissue for stapling.
The surgeon uses a Maryland Dissector to carefully dissect the pleura over the anterior hilum, visually identifying the superior pulmonary vein and the pulmonary artery.
The surgeon uses fenestrated forceps to maneuver around the superior pulmonary vein, demonstrating the articulated movement of the instrument with a complete ninety-degree angle in both directions.
The surgeon uses a blunt-tipped instrument to carefully dissect around large blood vessels, minimizing the risk of damage to these structures.
The surgeon identifies lymph nodes located behind the superior pulmonary vein, visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses fenestrated forceps to grasp lymph nodes behind the superior pulmonary vein at various angles, ensuring no damage. The bipolar Mary lens is then employed to gently dissect the lymph nodes from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon dissects around the anterior segmental pulmonary artery branch, carefully separating it from surrounding tissue using surgical instruments.
The surgeon alternates between using the bipolar Mary lens and fenestrated forceps to carefully maneuver around the pulmonary artery branch, effectively dissecting the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon has created a clear path around the anterior segmental pulmonary artery branch, preparing the area for the use of staplers.
The surgeon uses the Cavidian Tristapler with the Signia gun, introduced through the subxiphoid port, to fire staples around the anterior segmental pulmonary artery branch.
The surgeon begins dissection at the junction of the pericardium and surrounding fat tissue, carefully following the path of the phrenic nerve upwards.
The surgeon uses a bipolar Maryland dissector and fenestrated forceps to retract and dissect along the established plane, carefully manipulating the tissue.
The surgeon uses the bipolar Maryland dissector and fenestrated forceps to achieve optimal angles for dissection, enhancing visibility and access to the surgical site.
The surgical field provides a clear view of the innominate vein, rhema vein, and superior vena cava (SVC), highlighting their anatomical positions and relationships.
The surgeon uses instruments to dissect and remove fat tissue located between the innominate vein and the rhema vein, carefully separating the fat from these vascular structures.
The surgeon uses wristed instruments to perform subtle sweeping motions, facilitating precise dissection along the anatomical plane.
The tumor is visible above the surgical instruments, with the left superior vena cava (SVC) and the phrenic nerve lying over it.
The surgeon uses wristed Maryland dissectors and fenestrated forceps to navigate the tissue planes, with the instruments' angles aligning well with the anatomical structures during the thymectomy.
